one_line_takeaway: A DNS-inspired, PKI-anchored, protocol-agnostic registry specification (ANS) for secure, capability-aware AI-agent discovery across A2A/MCP/ACP, presented as a design with a MAESTRO threat analysis but no implemented benchmarks.
key_finding: The paper's central claim is architectural rather than measured: that a protocol-agnostic registry combining DNS-like structured naming, PKI-anchored verifiable identity, and a pluggable Protocol Adapter Layer can provide secure, capability-aware agent discovery that existing systems (DNS, DNS-SD, FIPA ACLs) and emerging protocols (A2A, MCP, ACP) do not offer on their own. It demonstrates feasibility through concrete schemas, resolution algorithms, and a systematic MAESTRO threat mapping showing each major discovery-layer threat has a corresponding PKI/validation-based mitigation. There are no experimental results, accuracy numbers, or performance benchmarks to report.
core_contribution: The paper proposes the Agent Name Service (ANS), a DNS-inspired, protocol-agnostic directory/registry protocol for secure discovery of AI agents. Its own contributions are: (1) a formal ANSName naming scheme structured as 'Protocol://AgentID.agentCapability.Provider.vVersion.Extension' with capability-aware, SemVer-based resolution; (2) a registry architecture with distinct roles (Requesting Agent, Agent Registry, Certificate Authority, Registration Authority, ANS service) that binds agent identity to PKI X.509 certificates; (3) a formalized agent lifecycle (registration via CSR, periodic renewal, deregistration/revocation via CRL/OCSP); (4) a modular Protocol Adapter Layer that normalizes protocol-specific metadata (A2A Agent Cards, MCP tool descriptions, ACP profiles) into a common 'protocolExtensions' container; (5) JSON Schema (draft-07) definitions for registration and capability request/response messages; (6) pseudocode algorithms for secure resolution, certificate-chain verification, signature verification, and version negotiation; and (7) a MAESTRO-framework-based threat analysis. It is a design/specification paper, not an empirical study. The paper explicitly distinguishes ANS from cited protocols: rather than replacing A2A/MCP/ACP, ANS is positioned as a complementary discovery-and-identity infrastructure layer beneath them.
threat_model: Adversary attempting to subvert agent discovery and trust in a multi-agent ecosystem. The paper structures threats via the MAESTRO 7-layer framework and enumerates concrete attacks with mitigations: (1) Agent Impersonation (adversary poses as a legitimate registered agent) - mitigated by mandatory PKI, certificate validation against a trusted CA, and proof-of-private-key-possession; (2) Registry Poisoning (injecting malicious agentCapability/Endpoint data) - mitigated by strict RA validation and cryptographically signed registry responses plus DB access controls; (3) Man-in-the-Middle (tampering with agent-agent/agent-registry traffic) - mitigated by digital signatures over responses and recommended mTLS transport; (4) DoS/DDoS against registry/RA/CA/resolution services - mitigated by distributed deployment, rate limiting, anycast; (5) Side-channel deanonymization of the querier's intent - mitigated by proposed Private Information Retrieval, anonymized query relays, differential privacy, and rate limiting; (6) Sybil attacks (mass fake identities) - mitigated by costly/hard registration. The paper also flags that DNSSEC-like signing can amplify DoS, so it recommends careful threat modeling before adopting it. Trust anchor is the Agent Registry's CA.
limitations: By the authors' own account this is a foundational proposal with no implemented, benchmarked system in the paper - performance, registration throughput, and scalability under load are explicitly left to future work. Governance is unresolved: preventing naming collisions/squatting requires an ICANN-like authority that does not yet exist, and dispute/fee/root-CA-stewardship models are only sketched. The ACP reference (IBM) is a placeholder for an anticipated publication, so one of the three target protocols is not yet concretely specified. Cross-protocol interoperability is limited to discovery and identity - ANS does not translate an A2A task into an MCP request, so deep semantic interoperability still needs additional gateways. Privacy protections (PIR, differential privacy, anonymized relays) and DNSSEC-like signing are recommended but not implemented or evaluated, and the paper notes DNSSEC-style signing can worsen DoS amplification. Several 'security features' in the tables (ZKP capability attestation with Groth16, gVisor/V8 sandboxing, OPA RBAC+ABAC) appear as illustrative example configurations, not as built or tested components.

quantitative_results: Recommended default Endpoint cache TTL: 300 seconds (5 minutes), adjustable by policy; resolvers MUST re-validate after TTL expiry (design recommendation, not a measurement)
Illustrative A2A rate-limit config in example listing: TokenBucket, refillRate 100/s, burstCapacity 500, per-capability (example config, not evaluated)
Illustrative MCP tool-sandbox config in example listing: gVisor environment, V8Isolate runtime, 256MB memory limit, 0.5 CPU quota, DENY_ALL network policy (example config, not evaluated)
Illustrative ZKP proof system named for capability verification: Groth16 (example config, not evaluated)
Agent IDs proposed as deterministic UUIDv5 derived from a hash of the PKI public key (design choice in Table II)
baselines_compared: DNS (RFC 1035) - conceptual baseline; provides name-to-address resolution but lacks capability granularity, verifiable identity, and lifecycle management
DNS-SD (RFC 6763) - conceptual baseline; adds local service discovery but no verifiable identity or global capability matching
FIPA Agent Communication Languages - conceptual baseline; influential in MAS but lacks built-in security and modern transport
A2A (Google), MCP (Anthropic), ACP (IBM) - positioned as complementary emerging protocols that ANS sits beneath, not empirical baselines

Implementation-pattern qualitative decision matrix (Table III): Centralized vs Distributed-Cassandra vs Distributed-DHT vs Blockchain/DLT vs Federated, rated on consistency, latency, scalability, fault tolerance, security, operational cost, complexity
